notes: Official party policy source inventory and manual structured rows. Add voter-facing policy rows only after manual review against the official source URL.

rows.headline: Immigration restriction and removals
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says it would freeze non-essential immigration, stop small boat crossings by leaving the ECHR, detain and deport illegal arrivals, restrict access to benefits, and introduce an employer immigration tax while exempting essential foreign health and care workers.
rows.detailedSummary: Freeze non-essential immigration, with essential skills mainly around healthcare as the exception.|Leave the ECHR as part of its small boats plan and detain and deport illegal arrivals.|Bar student dependants and limit routes that Reform says abuse immigration rules.|Require five years of residency and employment before claiming benefits.|Raise employer National Insurance for foreign workers, while exempting essential foreign health and care workers.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This is a highly restrictive immigration position that links border control to wages, housing pressure and public-service capacity.

rows.headline: Tax cuts, waste reduction and business deregulation
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says it would cut wasteful spending, raise the income tax starting threshold to £20,000, raise the higher-rate threshold to £70,000, cut energy taxes, reduce corporation tax, lift the VAT threshold to £150,000 and reduce regulation on business.
rows.detailedSummary: Require departments to reduce wasteful spending without touching frontline services.|Raise the income tax starting threshold to £20,000 and the higher-rate threshold to £70,000.|Cut residential stamp duty and abolish inheritance tax for estates under £2m.|Lift the minimum corporation tax profit threshold to £100,000 and reduce the main corporation tax rate.|Lift the VAT threshold to £150,000 and abolish IR35 rules.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This frames economic policy around tax cuts, spending restraint and deregulation, with the fiscal balance dependent on claimed savings and growth effects.

rows.headline: NHS waiting lists and healthcare capacity
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says the NHS should remain free at the point of delivery while being reformed to reduce waiting lists. Its pledges include tax relief for frontline NHS and social care staff, ending training caps for UK medical students, using independent healthcare capacity and introducing vouchers where NHS waits exceed set limits.
rows.detailedSummary: Keep healthcare free at the point of delivery while reforming the NHS to improve outcomes and reduce waiting lists.|Give frontline NHS and social care staff zero basic-rate tax for three years.|End training caps for UK medical students and write off student fees over NHS service.|Use independent and not-for-profit healthcare capacity in the UK and overseas.|Offer NHS patients a voucher for private treatment where GP, consultant or operation waits exceed stated limits.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This keeps a free-at-use NHS commitment but relies heavily on staffing tax incentives, management reform and independent-sector capacity.

rows.headline: Housebuilding, planning and local priority
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says it would increase home ownership by accelerating housebuilding, cutting immigration, fast-tracking brownfield development, changing social housing priority rules, restoring landlord finance-cost relief and using construction technology to speed up building.
rows.detailedSummary: Unleash housebuilding and cut immigration as part of its housing approach.|Fast-track planning and tax incentives for brownfield development.|Prioritise local people and those who have paid into the system in social housing law.|Restore landlords' rights to deduct finance costs and mortgage interest from rental income tax.|Use modular construction and digital technology to speed up building and reduce waste.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This combines supply-side planning changes with a strong local-priority frame and a direct link between housing pressure and immigration.

rows.headline: Curriculum, discipline and higher education reform
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says education should build skills, character and pride in Britain. Its pledges include a patriotic curriculum, banning transgender ideology in schools, tax relief on independent education, scrapping interest on student loans, more pupil referral units and requiring universities to offer two-year undergraduate courses.
rows.detailedSummary: Introduce a patriotic curriculum in primary and secondary schools.|Ban transgender ideology in primary and secondary schools and require single-sex facilities.|Apply 20% tax relief on independent education.|Scrap interest on student loans and extend repayment periods.|Double pupil referral units for violent and disruptive students.|Require universities to provide two-year undergraduate courses.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This frames education through cultural curriculum change, school discipline and reduced higher-education costs rather than a broad funding expansion.

rows.headline: Net zero rollback and domestic energy
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says net zero is raising bills and weakening industry. It would scrap net zero and related subsidies, tax renewable subsidies, fast-track North Sea oil and gas licences, test shale gas sites and pursue nuclear, lithium, gas, synthetic fuel, tidal power and clean coal options.
rows.detailedSummary: Scrap net zero and related subsidies.|Apply equivalent taxes to annual renewable energy subsidies.|Fast-track North Sea oil and gas licences.|Grant shale gas licences on test sites for two years, with local compensation schemes if production follows.|Fast-track small modular nuclear reactors and explore UK lithium, gas turbines, synthetic fuel, tidal power and clean coal.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This prioritises lower energy bills and domestic supply over the current net-zero timetable, while retaining some technology-led environmental measures.

rows.headline: Benefits conditionality and back-to-work reform
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says those who can work should work. Its benefits pledges include raising the income tax starting point to strengthen work incentives, reforming benefit support and training, applying a two-strike rule for job offers, and using face-to-face independent medical assessments for disability and work capability payments.
rows.detailedSummary: Raise the income tax starting point to £20,000 to make work pay and incentivise people on benefits into work.|Reform benefit support and training to help people back into work, with particular focus on 16-34 year olds.|Apply a two-strike rule where jobseekers and those fit to work must find employment within four months or accept a job after two offers, otherwise benefits are withdrawn.|Use face-to-face Personal Independence Payment and Work Capability Assessment checks.|Require independent medical assessments to prove eligibility, while exempting people with severe disabilities or serious long-term illnesses from regular checks.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This frames welfare mainly through work incentives, conditionality and assessment reform rather than through higher benefit entitlements.

rows.headline: Roads, HS2 and critical infrastructure
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says it would scrap the remaining HS2 project, ban ULEZ Clean Air Zones and Low Traffic Neighbourhoods, remove net-zero vehicle mandates, improve existing rail and road links, and create a new ownership model for utilities with 50% public ownership and 50% UK pension fund ownership.
rows.detailedSummary: Scrap the remaining HS2 project.|Legislate to ban ULEZ Clean Air Zones and Low Traffic Neighbourhoods.|Remove bans on petrol and diesel cars and legal requirements for manufacturers to sell electric cars as part of scrapping net zero policy.|Improve existing rail and road links, focusing on coastal regions, Wales, the North and the Midlands.|Create a new critical national infrastructure model with 50% public ownership and 50% ownership by UK pension funds.|Merge and overhaul infrastructure funding bodies and remove net-zero objectives from the infrastructure funding process.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This combines a pro-motorist transport position with a more interventionist approach to utilities and critical infrastructure ownership.

rows.headline: Constitutional reform and sovereignty
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says it would leave the European Convention on Human Rights, reform the Human Rights Act, replace the House of Lords with a smaller democratic second chamber, reform the Civil Service, restrict postal voting, support a referendum on proportional representation, and introduce a British Bill of Rights.
rows.detailedSummary: Leave the European Convention on Human Rights and reform the Human Rights Act to put the rights of law-abiding people first.|Replace the House of Lords with a smaller democratic second chamber.|End political appointees in the Civil Service and replace Civil Service leaders with successful professionals from the private sector as political appointees.|Restrict postal voting to elderly, disabled and housebound voters.|Support a referendum on proportional representation for the House of Commons.|Introduce a British Bill of Rights covering freedoms, privacy, surveillance and public accountability.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This puts sovereignty and institutional change at the centre, combining rights-law withdrawal with proposals for parliamentary, voting and civil-service reform.

rows.headline: Defence, Brexit sovereignty and foreign aid
rows.shortSummary: Reform UK says it would cut foreign aid by 50%, scrap retained EU regulations, abandon the Windsor Framework, resist EU defence integration, prepare to renegotiate the UK-EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement, and increase defence spending to 2.5% of national GDP by year three and 3% within six years.
rows.detailedSummary: Cut foreign aid by 50% and review the effectiveness of overseas aid and global bodies funded by the UK.|Scrap retained EU regulations with immediate effect.|Abandon the Windsor Framework, which Reform says leaves Northern Ireland too closely tied to EU rules.|Resist EU defence integration and protect independence for Britain's armed forces.|Prepare to renegotiate the UK-EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement.|Increase defence spending to 2.5% of national GDP by year three and 3% within six years, with an urgent armed forces pay review and a dedicated ministerial department for veterans.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This frames foreign policy through sovereignty, reduced aid spending, Brexit renegotiation and higher defence capacity rather than broader international-development commitments.

rows.headline: Healthcare and NHS access
rows.shortSummary: Restore Britain says the NHS should be restored as a national health service serving British people first. Its healthcare pledges include free parking for NHS staff, patients and visitors, removing medical school caps, and prioritising British-trained doctors.
rows.detailedSummary: Restore the NHS as a national health service serving British people first.|Offer free parking for NHS staff, patients and visitors.|Remove medical school caps and prioritise British-trained doctors.
rows.politiscopeAnalysis: This frames NHS pressure mainly through national access, workforce and migration rather than through private-sector reform.
